mapping.a_element: classical Hamiltonian phase-space dynamics of a bound, metastable configuration (the HCN isomerization well)
mapping.b_element: classical Hamiltonian dynamics of the Jovian planet system in its phase space

mapping.a_element: reactant lifetime / dynamical decay time of the HCN well
mapping.b_element: dynamical lifetime of Uranus (~10^18 yr) / Lyapunov time (~10^7 yr)

mapping.a_element: statistical (RRKM/transition-state) rate estimate assuming full phase-space mixing
mapping.b_element: classical analytic theory predicting a stable solar system

mapping.a_element: observed nonstatistical / nonexponential decay departing from the statistical prediction
mapping.b_element: observed chaotic behavior (from numerical integration) departing from the analytic stability prediction

brief_justification: Both are classical multidimensional Hamiltonian phase-space problems that quote a dynamical lifetime and contrast an analytic/statistical prediction with the true numerical dynamics, but their specific transferable machinery differs: paper A's mechanism is phase-space transition-state theory — flux through a dividing surface characterized by a gap-time/lifetime distribution — whereas paper B's mechanism is the resonance-overlap (Chirikov) criterion for the onset of chaos among mean-motion resonances. A dividing surface for reactive flux and a resonance-overlap chaos criterion are distinct objects with no clean role-for-role correspondence, so the shared content is the broad discipline and theme, not the governing mechanism.
